00_thesis/thesis-log.html.title: thesis-log
00_thesis/thesis-log.html.description:
2026-02-28
2026-03-01
2026-03-02
2026-03-04
https://lalith.in/2020/09/27/Low-Level-Advice-For-Systems-Research prepare your experiment infrastructure as early as possible goal of prototypes is to exercise a certain hypothesis automate experiment workflow as systems get complex, ensure correctness with tests avoid tunnel vision. write first few paragraphs of paper's evaluation section Compare results of experiments from the system's same version use feature flags check features individually
 John Ousterhout: “Use your intuition to ask questions, not answer them”. Log extensively. Recommended reads:
[x] “Performance Evaluation of Computer and Communication Systems”, Jean-Yves Le Boudec.
[x] “The Art of Computer Systems Performance Analysis: Techniques for Experimental Design, Measurement, Simulation, and Modeling”, Raj Jain.
[x] “Mathematical foundations of computer networking”, Srinivasan Keshav.
[ ] “Systems Benchmarking Crimes”, Gernot Heiser
 “Systems Benchmarking Crimes”, Gernot Heiser performance should improve significantly in the area of interest performance should not significantly degrade elsewhere demonstrate acceptability of new overheads pick experiments justly don't downplay/sugarcoat overheads show other stats, use geometric means split train and test (calibration and eval. it is caled) consider baseline performance carefully when running other app be sure it doesnt have debugging and stuff like that enabled. Be sure that current app is the best working version of this baseline artifact document what you are doing I have been thinking about keeping a log of my thoughts and progress on thesis. This documentation will also help creating a report. Here's a clean action plan from your meeting notes:## 📚 Literature Discovery - **Web of Science** – learn how to use it for systematic searches (Thomson/Clarivate) - **Scopus** – explore as a second database - **IEEE Xplore** – read papers specifically here for distributed systems ## 🗂️ Reading Strategy - Use LLMs to summarize papers quickly → decide: _is the core idea worth reading in full?_ - Focus on: what's the main contribution? is it worth deep reading? ## 🎯 Target Venue Research - Find **top-tier CS conference ranking websites** (e.g. [csrankings.org](https://csrankings.org/), [CORE Rankings](https://portal.core.edu.au/conf-ranks/), CCF list) - Look at **journals and conferences** as publication targets - Study **classification taxonomies** in distributed systems to map the research space ## 🕵️ Community Stalking (in a good way) - Browse conference **session titles and sub-topics** → identify active areas - **Stalk prolific authors** in those sessions → follow their recent work and research threads ## 🗓️ Participation - Research upcoming **conferences to attend** - Aim to actually **attend relevant conferences** for networking + topic awareness --- **Suggested order to start:** 1. CORE Rankings + CSRankings → identify your target venues 2. IEEE Xplore + Web of Science → start searching those venues 3. LLM-assisted triage → build a shortlist of candidate problems 4. Author stalking → find who's active in your candidate areas These are the simplified set of tasks extracted from last meeting with my professor. Throughout the research, artifact/deliverable oriented task setting approach should be embraced.
https://obsidian.md/plugins?id=webpage-html-export Will use this extension to export my logs and thesis related files into a html file easing out the research tracking process. As this whole 00_thesis folder can be exported. I am considering adding some dashboards in the future. I can even transfer my blog to this setup. It is really handy!

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
